# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I wanna be STRESSED OUT watching a movie!!

**Score:** 129
**Body:** Hereditary, The Babadook, As Above So Below, The Ritual (ESPECIALLY the first 3/4s of The Ritual when its just wide shots that make you THINK somethings happening OH FUUUCK I loved how badly that shit made my heart race), stuff like that is exactly what im looking for! 

Horror/thriller movies that dont lay everything out on the table for you and make your imagination do the work are THE BEST (thats why I hated the last quarter of The Ritual, it explained everything and just ruined the mystery of the movie) and ive been CRAVING smth new for a good while now 😭. For Hereditary I just loved the vibe they kept through the whoel thing, you just couldnt get a break from more trauma 😭

Another movie that scratched the itch was The Lighthouse surprisingly! I think it was the whole "what the actual fuck are these people saying and WHAT IS ON THAT LIGHTHOUSE" atmosphere that never let my brain have a break 😂 

Anyway, if you have anything that vaguely fits my description, Id be glad to give it a try! 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not horror, but Whiplash is non-stop stress! And a great movie.
2. Uncut Gems will stress you out in another way.
  - Ooooooh Im intrigued now haha putting it on my watch list!!!
    - Oh yea, for sure watch Uncut Gems. Gave me anxiety for the whole thing. It's one you remember, for good or bad
3. Beau is afraid. Anxiety attack the movie.
4. Speak No Evil (2022)
5. Apocalypto
6. Uncut gems, good time…. Both Safdie bros
7. Green room!
8. mother! gave me huge anxiety. might work better if you’re a woman though

## 2. Being there (1979) is a fantastic movie starring Peter Sellers as Chance, a simple-minded gardener who is mistaken for a wealthy and influential man.

**Score:** 104
**Body:** Being There is a 1979 American satirical comedy-drama film starring Peter Sellers, Shirley MacLaine, and Melvyn Douglas. 

Directed by Hal Ashby, it is based on the 1970 novel Being There by Jerzy Kosiński, and adapted for the screen by Kosiński and the uncredited Robert C. Jones. 

Jack Warden, Richard Dysart, and Richard Basehart are featured in support. Its theme is an empty suit who the entire system unwittingly supports as president.

Douglas won the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actor and Sellers was nominated for Best Actor. The screenplay won the British Academy Film Award for Best Screenplay and the Writers Guild of America Award for Best Comedy Adapted from Another Medium. It was also nominated for the Golden Globe Award for Best Screenplay. 

In 2015, the Library of Congress selected Being There for preservation in the National Film Registry, finding it "culturally, historically, or aesthetically significant". It has since developed a strong cult following and is a favorite among many filmmakers.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hal Ashby directed Harold and Maude, the Last Detail, Shampoo, Coming Home and Being There in a row. How's that for a hot streak?
2. I absolutely love this movie. One of my favourites, and Peter Sellers is excellent in this. Time for a rewatch :)
  - The ending always got me thinking 
3. "I like to watch Eve."
4. Good film. I think the big house was the largest residential home in the US at the time.
  - It still is though it’s a tourist place and not a home they live in anymore. 

Biltmore Estate in Asheville, NC
5. It's a movie unlike any other I've ever seen, I think. Its appeal is hard to describe, but I was captivated almost immediately. 
Really have to watch it again, sometime soon!
6. There's a lot of "Management Consultants" who follow the Being There template, where inane observations are mistaken for amazing insights.
7. Honestly one of the best movies ever made. 

"I'll go get Warren."
8. Thank you _very_ much for posting this. Being There absolutely blew me away the first time I saw it 30+ years ago, and it's been in my all-time top 10 since then. (I'm a huge Peter Sellers fan; Dr. Strangelove is another of my top 10.) The final scene on the lake is easily one of the best endings in cinema history.

I've recommended it to many people over the years, but I'm not sure where it's currently streaming. If you can find it, it's an absolute must see film!

## 3. Movies that take place in a very “exaggerated” world?

**Score:** 35
**Body:** I just watched Brazil and I absolutely loved it. One thing I thought was great about it was the strange world it was set in and how bureaucracy was a major thing in this universe 

Something like cyberpunk as a property is similar. Capitalism taken to its most extreme, with only two companies effectively owing everything

Are there other movies with that same idea?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Fifth Element
2. Blade Runner
3. Idiocracy  
Harrison Bergeron
4. The Lobster
5. Equilibrium
6. Don't miss The Fisher King by the same director. The world is recognizable, but it's my favorite movie by Gilliam.
7. Big Fish...since I haven't seen it mentioned yet
8. Poor Things
9. Poor Things
10. The Truman Show

## 4. Based in true story

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I’m looking for movies that are based on true stories, and or are accurately depicting the time the story takes place. Such as proper clothing, weapons, etc.

Historical true stories would be absolutely banger but I’m open to anything as long as it’s based on true stories, these seem to make me have more of an emotional response to films. Thanks.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Downfall (2004)
2. Lost Bus (2025) was gripping. Admittedly a little cheesy at first but it takes off like a rocket.
3. Zodiac (2009)
Rivers Edge (1985)
4. If you like baseball--

*Moneyball* (2011)
  - You don't even need to know a thing about baseball to love this movie.
5. Maybe this isn't what you're looking for since you mentioned accurate weapons and clothing, but from what I understand Apollo 13 is pretty accurate. 
  - That’s a really good one!
6. In Cold Blood.     

The Untouchables.      

Schindlers List      

42.    

Raging Bull
7. Heavenly Creatures *(1994)*

A well-researched film based on a notorious murder which took place in New Zealand in 1954.  The filmmakers interviewed everyone they could find who were there at the time. They used actual diary entries and went out of their way to cast actors who actually resembled their characters. They also filmed scenes in actual locations whenever possible. Directed by Peter Jackson of *Lord of the Rings* fame. It's definitely a movie that lingers with you long after it's over.
8. Pride (2014) Gay activists support striking coal miners in Thatcher’s England.   Bill Nighy is one of the stars. There was some added bonus materials where people who were actual participants commented that the movie did a good job representing the people.

## 5. Fun edgy shock value indies for dude movie night. Please suggest based off of my list

**Score:** 20
**Body:** The types of films I'm talking about, please try and suggest based off of my list:

Tusk

Gummo

Happiness

The Substance

Sorry To Bother You

Swiss Army Man

Upgrade

Hobo With A Shotgun

The Greasy Strangler

Election

Creep

Bad Boy Bubby





### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Bubba Ho-Tep
2. Freaked

The Doom Generation

Filth

Poultrygeist

Bronson

The Boxer's Omen
3. Green Room

Guns Akimbo

Turbo Kid

Versus
4. Death Proof

The Lobster

Black Sheep (2006 NZ)
  - i was buzzed when i saw black sheep. it was hilarious
5. Pink Flamingos
6. *Freeway* (1997)  
*Burn* (2019)  
*Black Snake Moan* (2006)
7. Guns Akimbo
8. Natural Born Killers

Butt Boy
9. Dead Alive

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. If you had to have an honorable death, what moment in cinema captures how you would want to go down?

**Score:** 623
**Body:** Obviously Aragorn doesn’t die here. But I watched Fellowship with several ppl that never read the books who def thought he was dying when they saw what he was up against 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I still love Boromir’s death scene (that might have made a better still for this post).

“I would have followed you, my brother, my captain, my king!”

(Got chills just writing that!)
2. “Hello boys, I’m baaaaaaaaaaacccccckkkkkk” from ID4.

Edit 1: ID4 was the official abbreviation from ads, some of the movie posters, and was used on the VHS box art. ID4 on top with the full name directly below it. Idk why, but it’s what they did. I was ~14 when it came out and have always referred to it this way or by the full title interchangeably.

Edit 2: mostly pain free, global hero who literally saved the world and countless lives, redeemed in the eyes of your children, what better way could there be to go?
3. “Shut the fuck up, and let me die in peace”
4. Gordy's gone, man
  - I’ll be outside
5. Bill Paxton went down like a badass in Aliens after being a little bitch the entire movie.
  - Something I love about his character is that despite his bullshit (as Ripley so eloquently puts it) the dude stands and delivers. Not just at the end but throughout the movie.

-He jumps right up to help Hicks with the xeno at the APC door and gets the shit acid-burned out of his arm in the process

-He puts himself between Newt and the face hugger in the infirmary and blasts it

-He leaves The Alamo to sweep for xenos with Vasquez

-And finally he goes down fighting at the end. I'm pretty sure his last scripted words (other than "AAAAAAH") were him screaming *"fuck you!"* while blasting the xeno that grabbed him through the floor in the face with the last of his 10mm ammo.

He's absolutely a stand in for the audience, losing his shit and bemoaning their inevitable doom, but he fights too.
6. “This is from Matilda.”
7. Realistically?  Murph from interstellar 

She died surrounded by her family, got an emotional goodbye with the one person that she felt betrayed by, lived to an old age, was accomplished in her field, saved the human race and will be put down into the history books forever.  Most likely she died mostly painlessly because of the drugs they put her on in the hospital bed so she could see Cooper.
8. “I've seen things you people wouldn't believe. Attack ships on fire off the shoulder of Orion. I watched C-beams glitter in the dark near the Tannhäuser Gate. All those moments will be lost in time, like tears in rain. Time to die.” -Roy Batty

## 2. Who else is the best Black actor if not Denzel Washington?

**Score:** 222

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Morgan Freeman is a contender.
  - Morgan Freeman, Forest Whitaker, Sydney Poitier, James Earl Jones, and Louis Gossett Jr. all seem like contenders
2. Everyone forgets about Laurence Fishburne.

Dude is amazing.
3. [removed]
  - what do you mean, "you people!"???
    - I’m just a dude, playing a dude, disguised as another dude! you’re a dude who don’t know what dude he is!
4. Sidney Poitier
5. Andre Braugher was awesome!  Mostly a tv actor but he was amazing as Frank Pembleton on Homicice and Capt. Holt on Brooklyn 99.
6. Djimon Hounsou is criminally underrated, at least here in America.  When that man shouts, I sit down
7. Mahershala Ali - 2 Oscars

## 3. Best film quadrilogy?

**Score:** 161
**Body:** The Lethal Weapon series is great. Four films, same director throughout, plenty of thrills, spills and in-jokes linking the films in succession. Not a single bad entry among the 4.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "Your baby is having my baby. And your baby, whoa baby"
  - Bloody marvelous 
2. This is actually the best answer.  I can’t come up with anything else that went with annnd ended up with 4 installments.

Edit: maaaaybe the Evil Dead series but ehh it’s not even close for engagement and actually wanting the heroes to win.
3. Jason Bourne.
  - I hate the conflict you create in me saying this lol
4. Toy Story. The forth film was completely unnecessary but it was still a very good movie.
5. Mad Max.

The Road Warrior and Fury Road are the heavy hitters. The original is sadly relevant in its depiction of a society slowly breaking down.

Beyond Thunderdome is controversial, but I like it. I don't mind the lost boys tribe that pops up. I think they add a lot to the world building. There are campy elements in Bartertown that I think are funny. Mostly I don't mind George Miller wanting to do something different. And the ending is a better conclusion to the series than the endings of either Road Warrior or Fury Road.
6. John Wick
7. “we’re not too old for this shit.”
8. Alien - on average the movies are good. The first two are exceptional, three is okay, and well four just makes us appreciate the first ones that much more…

## 4. Actors that always make you enjoy their performance, even when the movie may be bad

**Score:** 148
**Body:** They just have that thing, even when you can't quite name it (their facial expressions, body language, their line delivery or articulation, way too much passion or dedication, perhaps), that you just enjoy the way they portrait their characters, always, and even when the movie they are in is not that great or A rated

4 personal examples in the image above but also Rachel McAdams, Joseph Gordon-Levitt and Matthew McConaughey are way up there too for me

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. As usual, for not adding information to the picture OP can eat shit and live:

Jake Gyllenhaal, Kang Ha-neul, Emma Stone, and Viola Davis.
  - Much appreciated., didn't know Kang Ha-neul
  - Thank you!
2. Paul Giamatti. One of our best living actors. He may be in bad movies once in a while but he will never, EVER, give a bad performance.
  - John Adams is kick ass. He nails it.
3. Nick Cage is a big one
4. Christoph Waltz
5. LaKeith Stanfield
6. Coincidence, I’m actually watching Prisoners which stars Jake and Viola. Phenomenal movie and nowhere near bad!
7. Jim Carrey and Robin Williams for me.

## 5. What film have you watched too many times for no reason?

**Score:** 106
**Body:** The film is fine. It has some good vibes but for some reason I watch a film at least 10 times when I was 16.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The mummy!
2. Jurassic Park, the thing, and the abyss
3. "For your eyes only.". 80's Bond flick.  That was the summer we got cable.
4. Reign of Fire 2003
  - Just found out about that movie this year and have already re-watched it lol. McConaughey and Bale are two of my favourite actors, and there’s dragons. Was dumbfounded that I hadn’t heard of it
5. Fire & Ice and heavy metal, normally back to back
6. Die Hard. 

Saw it 26 or so times in the theater. Watch it every once in a while for fun and at Christmas, of course.
7. I have watched Matrix so often that now it just takes me 16 minutes to watch the whole movie when skipping to the next scene once I've seen "enough".
8. The Town
9. Mean Girls and Superbad.. did not know why

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Masculine and Feminine Urges *28 Years Later*

**Score:** 24
**Body:** The white-knuckle first act of Danny Boyle’s *28 Years Later* is about a boy trying and failing to become a man. Spike accompanies his father Jamie into infected territory—it’s his first venture of this sort and constitutes a rite of passage in the community. The filmic style is accordingly teeming with masculinity. 

Evoking raw trail camera footage, Boyle intersperses the father and son’s journey with brief cuts to infrared video of the infected predating animals and marking territory. A non-diegetic reading of Rudyard Kipling’s “Boots,” a chilling war poem that plays like a drill sergeant yelling at marching soldiers equally yells at the audience. It crescendos with the guttural, siren-like score to leave the viewer increasingly unnerved and uneasy. 

Archival footage of men at war from long before the outbreak is juxtaposed with the present day video game KillCam style editing upon contact of an arrow penetrating an infected (a natural yet bleeding edge evolution of Boyle’s distinctive frenetic style, complete with the use of iPhones to capture). The comparison between the old and the new shows that nothing has changed from *28 Years Ago*, or *28 Centuries Ago* for that matter: it still is and always has been “people killing people.” As Kipling asserts over and over in his poem, “There’s no discharge in the war!” Having no concerns with self-effacement in classic Danny Boyle fashion, he is at the top of his game with the brash and bold direction on display here. 

So much so that *28 Years Later* winds up feeling rather disjointed when he lets his foot off the gas. By the film’s close, screenwriter Alex Garland has flipped the script. If the first act is an all masculine affair about death and destruction, what follows embraces femininity and celebrates life. Spike leaves home again, but this time with his mother Isla. Spike cares less about killing the infected and more about saving his mother from a different kind of disease—one that requires not fighting with fire but handling with care. They even manage to have some fun along the way, making goofy faces at each other and sharing laughs despite the dire circumstances. Isla asks Spike if his father is silly with him too, to which he simply replies in the negative. His father is good at keeping him alive but not making him feel safe.  

Spike does his level best to make his mother feel protected in a way that his father failed to do for him. In her confused state due to her ailment, Isla refers to her own son as “dad.” This title, however inaccurate, pronounces that the boy has become a man through taking care of his family. But it’s a much different, more compassionate, and even almost feminine kind of masculinity to the one he sought and fell short of in the first act with his father. 

During the course of their journey, Spike and Isla stumble upon a female infected who’s in the process of giving birth. Pregnancy has seemingly rendered this infected nonthreatening and possibly even provided a degree of consciousness. She allows her fellow maternal presence in Isla to help deliver her baby girl, who inexplicably appears to be a normal, uninfected human—there are miracles in maternity, birth, and life. 

The baby’s infected father is about to kill Spike when he is saved by the doctor he sought in the hopes of a cure for Isla’s condition. But the doctor does not kill the infected. He upholds his Hippocratic Oath even for the infected despite the collapse of the institutions he swore it to. The doctor shows Spike and Isla his Bone Temple where he keeps the skulls of the deceased—the infected and uninfected alike. He commemorates the memories and lives of everyone in opposition to Spike’s father who maintained that the infected were mindless and soulless and therefore lifeless. When the doctor determines that the mother‘s disease is not one that can be fought, he facilitates an assisted suicide, giving her agency and dignity in her last decision in life. 

What holds *28 Years Later* back is a problem of synthesis. The film winds up feeling tonally confused with somewhat jarring turns that occasionally verge on absurdity. It’s somewhat unclear if this was the intent, namely during the emotional climax when it becomes clear that Isla has opted to die without hesitation. Spike is then almost immediately handed his deceased mother’s pared off skull. 

Is it supposed to be emotional and resonant or absurd and funny? You would think it’s the former, but one can never be sure given the execution and especially due to the way Boyle and Garland have chosen to end the film which is unabashedly intended to be over the top and humorous. Regardless of whether they flawlessly stuck the landing, Boyle and Garland only returned *28 Years* after their original *28 Days Later* about the readily regressive and transgressive nature of men because they still have more interesting—and nuanced—ruminations about gender and much more, even when society has long since collapsed.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Garland's interview about this film on Script Apart podcast was very much worth a listen. Maybe even more than you'd think, he reveals his condescension to Jamie as a character and his relative reverence for Kelson.

Part of the importance of Kelson to Garland seems to have been the character's inception, after several draft screenplays, as the aspect of the story that would make the whole thing "weird" enough for Boyle. 

In this interview Garland describes Kelson (I'm paraphrasing) as a "reverse Kurtz" who has been civilised rather than barbarised by extremes of the unfamiliar and unknown. And explicitly contrasts Kelson with Jamie.

Anyway, while all this is highly gendered and deserves to be read as such, for Garland it seems to have been more about bringing to light a spectrum of worldly affects, manners of human openness and closure, different ways in which and spirits with which to encounter events. He mentions the crucial (and surprising) carriage scene with the Infected as having been intent on this objective as well. 

Garland is a clever writer because the screenplay delivers a somewhat banal, even what you might call a default judgement of the relative value of these ways of being, but it repeatedly brings its judgement forward in an unexpected and poignant way that revives the power of simple ideas.
  - Thank you for the very insightful response! Couldn’t agree more with everything you said and I will absolutely check out the Script Apart podcast to hear more about what you mentioned from Garland, appreciate the recommendation!
2. I think you're picking holes that aren't really there with regard to his mother's death. They make it clear that there's no cure and she's only going to deteriorate, so she chooses to die, and Alfie learns to accept her choice. Obviously it's not supposed to be absurd and funny.

I'm not sure it's overwhelmingly a film about gender either. Certainly the society is built around traditional gender roles, but that's just an element in the film, not the main theme. I'd say it's a more general theme of how the world has developed since the original film.

I've heard a couple of interviews with Boyle and Garland, and they haven't mentioned the gender thing particularly. That's not to say it isn't there, but there's a risk that if you choose to see everything through a particular lens, you'll find evidence to back up your theory, but it'll be a distorted view of the film.
  - Thanks for taking the time to read and respond! I tend to agree that Garland’s intent wasn’t to be absurd and funny from a story perspective, I suppose my issue is with the execution—the tone and the way the sequence was edited seemed odd to me and I honestly wouldn’t be entirely surprised if Boyle recognized the absurdity of it all and leaned into it. Also didn’t mean to suggest that it’s only or mainly about gender, obviously there’s Brexit and much more but I recognize I don’t have as interesting things as others do to say about those other themes so this is just the aspect of the film I wanted to write about. I don’t think you have to only write about what a film is mainly about, somewhat peripheral topics can be interesting too and inform the more main themes
3. I really appreciate this reading of the film. I agree that the beginning felt hypermasculine/toxic masculinity but I love this take that it then turns to more of an embrace of femininity and more of a feminine take of being where aspects of community and care are what turns him from boy to man. 

I also agree with the reading of the skull scene, it did feel a little absurd and while watching it I wasn’t sure what to make of it when I watched it. In the moment I tried to appreciate it as a way of looking at a different cultural practice that humans may partake in 28 years from now.

I think for me the ending is what makes the film stumble and lose itself, I didn’t like the comic ending after such a strong serious film, and it didn’t feel like the ending for the film I was watching.
  - That’s exactly how I felt - the skull scene and the ending were the things I didn’t quite “get” in a film that I found otherwise was full of very interesting themes and ideas. Thanks for contributing to the discussion :)

## 2. Phantasm (1979): A Lynchian film before Lynch.

**Score:** 12
**Body:** [spoilers]

Have you noticed Coscarelli's Phantasm elevates when it's interpreted with dream logic rather than just being a slasher horror movie? In fact it reminds me a lot of Mulholland Drive, and it's not that Lynch owns the surreal type of filmmaking, but his work helped me appreciate other films for the gem that they are.

Phantasm follows a pair of brothers who amidst the mourning of a close friend, they're haunted by a creepy mortician and different evil creatures. But the biggest plot twist is that everything took place in the dreams of the younger sibling. So it's not far-fetched that it's a film charged with intriguing symbolism, archetypes and repressed memories of its protagonist.

The dream contains allegories for violence, lost of innocence, manhood rites of passage, and overwhelming thoughts that the consciousness of the dreamer cannot handle directly. However, death and abandonment are the main antagonists, as we see the two brothers constantly trying to save eachother from the Tall Man, an old creepy man who sort of reassemblea them psychically, he seems to be the amalgamation of their family's tragedy and the future that waits ahead.

By the third act the town is pitch black, we see an ominous white house, an overturned ice truck, windy landscapes and dark little creatures. Coscarelli frames the younger brother in front of a house chimney that looks like it's engulfing him in fire. The nightmare becomes more chaotic as his 'shadow' seeps in and becomes aware of his faith.

I have a personal theory that the core of protagonist's trauma is sexual violence but that would take a whole essay to explain, 

    what do you guys think of this film?



### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I appreciate your analysis of Phantasm, and the comparison to Lynch. I've heard others remark about the dreamy feeling of the film, with its feeling of emptiness.

Just wanted to point out though that Lynch's first film Eraserhead was released in 1977, before Phantasm.
2. I think you are correct that both Lynch and Coscarelli offer a dream like atmosphere and logic to their films although I think they both drew inspiration from others and their own styles more than each other.

In Italy, Brava, Fulci and early Argento were all making films that were embracing a dream like logic quality which in a lot of cases meant that visual storytelling and style trumped the script….Not that there’s anything wrong with that and the Italians were dealing with holdover fascist censoring plus crazy amounts of restrictions on time given to actually shoot the films.

Coscarelli was extremely budget restrained and he freely admits his scripts came second to the overall feeling and visuals of his films. He manages to make an incredibly compelling film in 1 and arguably 2/3 before losing it on the follow up sequels

Coscarelli seems to be happy enough to do his own thing with limited resources IIRC John Dies at the End was his biggest budget film (which I absolutely loved) 

Lynch went on to bigger things 

## 3. Is editing the most important part of filmaking?

**Score:** 10
**Body:** Directors and Screenwriters and people who shoot cinematography are all pretty important, don’t get me wrong, but the editor is the one who actually decides which shots are good and which shots are bad, which music choices are are right for the film and which are bad. They decide which parts of the film are worth being in the final cut and everything that makes it into the final cut, goes through them. They are essentially the final executives of the film, kinda like co directors. I don’t understand why people give directors so much credit honestly when they rely completely on their editors. Most directors say it’s the most important part of film making.

Editors literally control the style of the entire film. It’s an extremely meticulous process, easily the most technical that exists. A cut can be off by milliseconds, and it cannot work as a result. If the editing is bad then the entire movie is bad. It’s easily the most important job in the entire process.

I believe the reason why editing is so underrated is because most editors are women, most great ones at least like the ones for Martin Scorsese and David Lynch, not to mention Quentin Tarantino.

I literally can’t understand how much it matters, why do the people not appreciate it enough?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In a sense, yes, as editing is the only art form that is *unique* to film. This dates back to the Soviet montage theory, but Kubrick put it very well in an interview with Rolling Stone in 1987:

"Everything else comes from something else. Writing, of course, is writing, acting comes from the theater, and cinematography comes from photography. Editing is unique to film. You can see something from different points of view almost simuluneously, and it creates a new experience. Pudovkin gives an example: You see a guy hanging a picture on the wall. Suddenly you see his feet slip; you see the chair move; you see his hand go down and the picture fall off the wall. In that split second, a guy falls off a chair, and you see it in a way that you could not see it any other way except through editing. TV commercials have figured that out. Leave content out of it, and some of the most spectacular examples of film art are in the best TV commercials."
  - While I do agree that comparing "movie editing" to "book editing" isn't a great exercise -- the verb might be the same, but it describes really different actions -- I don't think that montage, that is to say the juxtaposition of images, is quite unique to film making. Poetry juxtaposes images all the time, and so does prose. The way free association works in *Un Chien Andalou* isn't that different from an Apollinaire poem. There's also the case of 20th Century and contemporary literature that explicitly parallels film-montage in its narrations and descriptions. You could even say that a [triptych](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triptych) is form of (static) montage.       
    
Of course trying to pin down the medium specificity of cinema is a tale a old as cinema itself. What is the one thing that makes cinema? As you said below, Eisenstein and much of the Soviets believed that thing to be montage. But perhaps the uniqueness of cinema is that it's a bit of a super-art, something that incorporates specific aspects of other art forms, using and amplifying them to constructs its own specificity. I think [Alain Badiou](https://www.samwoolfe.com/2024/09/badiou-zizek-cinema.html) talked about it in its own volume on cinema.  
     
So maybe it's not that montage is exclusive or unique to cinema, but it is the one where it matters the most. [This piece](https://www.lrb.co.uk/the-paper/v47/n19/john-lahr/every-blink) on the LRB about Walter Murch puts it quite well, I think:    
     
>If Murch is full of wonder at film’s storytelling possibilities, the inventors of the moving picture were not. ‘The cinema is an invention without a future,’ Louis Lumière declared. [...] The breakthrough, which turned a 19th-century novelty into the 20th century’s only new art form, was the arrival of montage in 1901. The transition from one shot to another transformed motion pictures from a literal medium into a psychological and poetic one. Movies could now jump back and forth in time and space, ‘the cinematic equivalent to the discovery of flight’, as Murch sees it. Out of its illusion of naturalistic flow – 24 frames a projected second – a new grammar of seeing and of storytelling evolved: close-ups, dissolves, long shots, fade-outs.
>        
>‘“Filmic” juxtapositions are taking place in the real world not only when we dream but also when we are awake,’ Murch wrote in his book from 1992, In the Blink of an Eye. This explains why audiences find edited film a surprisingly familiar experience. Every blink is a thought. Every thought is a cut. [...] ‘Look at that lamp across the room. Now look back at me. Look back at that lamp. Now look back at me again. Do you see what you did? You blinked. Those are cuts. Your mind cut the scene. First you behold the lamp. Cut. Then you behold me.’ In cinema, Murch says, ‘at the moment you decide to cut, what you are saying is, in effect, “I am going to bring this idea to an end and start something new.”’
  - One doesn't really follow from the other - just because it's unique doesn't mean that it's the most important.
2. >I don’t understand why people give directors so much credit honestly when they rely completely on their editors

Because competent director makes a rough edit of the movie while shooting it and after principal photography is done, oversees editing process? Good editor will always add to the movie, but if you were to make for example Scorsese edit his own movie it's not like it would fall apart
3. You you said that bad editing can ruin a film, but every element can ruin a film. Bad lighting can ruin a film bad acting certainly ruins the film bad casting dooms a film before it’s even shot. Bad, locations, bad production, design, bad, wardrobe bad, hair bad makeup it all can ruin a film.

For a great film you absolutely need a great script and a great cast. Once you have those two a film can be great.  A good editor can craft a good film. Once he has those two elements.  so much can be done in post production acting can be refined story can be paced. 
The editor is the person that builds the final product but it’s a collaborative effort.
4. Every element of filmmaking is the most important part of filmmaking.  A movie doesn't exist without a screenplay, except in rare cases that are better left unmentioned.  Cinematography is literally the part that makes it a movie.  The list goes on.  Hell, without the catering department, cast and crew would be dropping like flies by the end of the day.

Editing is one of the more underappreciated aspects of filmmaking, though, right up there with sound design.  I think it's because most people don't really understand it.  You usually don't even notice the edit unless something is wrong, and that's kind of the whole point.
5. You're not wrong that editing is extremely important and might even be the most important. But I reject your premise that "the editor decides" because the director is (or at least can be) present during those decisions and works with the editor. It's not like the director's work is done after shooting and the editor takes over on their own (unless there is a special case where the studio takes the movie out of the director's hands).
6. I think in general most people do not know exactly what the differing roles actually do in film making. I also think due to the shifting roles the director may take on exactly who should get the credit for whichever scene or moment in a movie.

Most of the time, as far as i am aware, the director would be sitting in with the editor on important scenes, if not ever scene discussing things and the editors job is to execute what the director wants, not take control of the film. However this again could vary. I remember in the directors commentary for Lucky Number Slevin the director praising the editor and saying they deserve all the credit for the chess game sequence because until they crafted it together the director had no idea how to do it. However the director presumably was on set ensuring those shots were done, so the multiple takes with the multiple actors could be sewn together seamlessly. I can not find the sequence to link to it but it is very well done moving seamlessly between three difference people at two different moments.

I do think it would be a nice experiment if several directors made the exact same base script, with different editors, music, production, cinematography staff just so there would be a practical demonstration of how much these choices effect the final film. How many would think to cast the same actor(s) but tell them to play the character in a different way etc. Would any change the tone to lean more into suspense than drama etc. Remakes are one thing but they are also quite removed from the original either by setting, or time period, or the original is on a pedestal so it is hard to really give another version a 'first' watch without instantly comparing the two. The closest we can get to this in a practical sense is differing productions of a play but even then unless the play is purposely trying to be different, it is not 'too' different from the 'original' production you have seen even if it is just to keep with the expectations of the story the audience has. Romeo and Juliet is a fairly light hearted tragedy, so a Spielberg version would no doubt be romantic with some pathos, A Tarantino version would likely lean heavier into the shady aspects of the warring families and not focus on the sentimentality, and the Lynch version no doubt would involve multiple acid trip like scenes exploring the nature of teenage hormones.
7. >I believe the reason why editing is so underrated is because most editors are women

Why would this be a reason for it to be underrated? 

As an editor myself, I think it's just underappreciated, but I wouldn't give it the title of most important. I think the script is *always* the deciding factor.   
  
There has never been a great movie without a great script, but there have been great movies with not so great editing, because people usually don't tend to notice that part of the process. 

>the editor is the one who actually decides which shots are good and which shots are bad

The editor is the one who decides, but what if there's not a good choice? Editing cannot save a terribly planned and shot movie beyond a certain point.
8. Cinema is image, therefore the most important person in a film is the one who thinks the images and the one who thinks the images is the director, he is the one who has the ideas, the other professionals (who are essential) execute the director's idea. The script can be great, the actors excellent, the technical team top-notch, but if the director doesn't have the ability to think the scenes well, the film will be bad and that's what happens most often.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. "I wonder if we're supposed to understand what they are saying right now"

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I have been rewatching a lot of my all time favorites with my beloved girlfriend recently and this is a thought I've had a disproportionate amount of times. Kill Bill and Dreamers being examples that immediately jump out in my mind; with no subtitles being available, am I supposed to understand what those gays are saying in French? And while I am a big fan of the Adam Friedland show, I do not speak Chinese and don't exactly understand what that monk guy was screaming at my childhood crush. And yes it was very impressive of me to just know what some lines meant from memory, but I do wanna know, (obviously the intent would vary director to director) what would you say should be the default assumption given that a character speaks a language that is not the "main" one of the movie for a prolonged period of time? Am I supposed to scuttle to my puter and enable subtitles or just vibe it out? 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. One thing I have noticed on streaming sites is that sometimes movies that did have subtitles on foreign-language scenes no longer have subtitles there unless you go and turn the subtitle setting on. It's frustrating.


(And I suspect that in some cases the original text may have been replaced with the dreaded [Speaks Spanish])
2. The fun thing about this is that it wasn’t a problem at all until shitty streaming services took over the world :) I don’t own Kill Bill on DVD so I can’t speak to the other comment example, but it’s usually pretty straightforward that either the dialogue is considered incidental/unimportant/you get the gist from context, so no need to translate, or actually it’s super important that you understand what German characters say in Inglorious Bastards, so of course there’s subtitles. There’s some exceptions, but it should always be intentional, ex: Spielberg’s West Side Story has a lot of Spanish dialogue that you just miss out on if you don’t speak Spanish, but the Puerto Rican characters keeping their language alive is a core theme of the movie, so it makes sense.

The problem is that streaming services (I think Amazon Prime was the first/most guilty?) operate in all countries, and work on a binary of do you want subtitles on or off. If you say on, it’s on for everything, in every language. If you say off, it’s off for everything. If your subtitles are turned off, you can play a foreign film, let’s say a German production with 100% of dialogue in German, and the movie will play without subtitles. I watched Anatomy of a Fall on Amazon Prime and as far as I could tell, they literally don’t have the option to play it with subtitles for French dialogue but not for English dialogue (which is of course how it was released in American theaters).
3. Kill Bill's subtitles seem to be off by default on my DVD version. However if you turn them on for the scenes where they speak Japanese, it does translate them. Other versions of the film may do it differently though. .

Other times you can work out what they're saying from the context. One Battle After Another has some unsubtitled Spanish, but they're pretty much saying "we're helping this guy, get those people out of here" which you could guess from watching it.

Then there's times where it's best to leave them out. In The French Connection Part 2, Gene Hackman's character doesn't speak French, so we get no subtitles, to help put us in his position.
  - Yeye, like if it's clearly intentional that you or the protagonist are supposed to feel lost in the situation (Victoria, Vanilla Sky), or that it's understandable from context clues (The Mummy) - I have no question. But I'm looking a "rule of thumb" for, like I said, prolonged periods of time where they just speak a different language and it doesnt seem to concern the protagonist. Your rule of thumb appears to be the default settings on a DVD - that's it? 

I'm just thinking, how would they show it in theaters? Those don't usually subtitles except for rare occurrences where it's clearly baked into the movie - so I'm guessing I'm supposed to just vibe it out?
    - I've seen Kill Bill a few times in different formats - although not the original theatrical release - and as far as I remember, most (if not all) of the non-English dialogue was subtitled. 

Given that there are some quite long scenes with Japanese dialogue, and some of it is quite integral to the plot, I find it hard to believe it wasn't supposed to be subtitled for English audiences. I suspect the lack of default subs on the DVD is a mistake.
    - Yes, the rule of thumb is that if the audience needs to understand it, the subtitles are supposed to be there. But sometimes technical issues with DVDs or streaming services mean that they may not be.
4. I’ve had the experience a few times where I did not have subtitles and later learned that I was supposed to, and genuinely enjoyed the viewing experience as it was. In Snowpiercer I had no subtitles for the Korean, which made the long scene in which the designer explains to the main character in Korean the story of what happened to him even more heartrending, since without subtitles not only does Evans’ character not understand this man’s soliloquy but neither do we. And in Rumours not knowing with Alicia Vikander is saying in Swedish fit perfectly with the entire atmosphere of the film. 

So I guess I “vibe it out” 🤷🏻‍♀️

---
